topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mahogany door clicked shut with a finality that scraped against my nerves. I stood there, my knuckles white where I gripped the brass handle, the silence of the empty office amplifying the sheer audacity of the situation. Professor Alistair, my guiding star for a decade, had simply vanished. No note. No farewell. Just an unanswered phone and an administrative memo confirming his 'retirement.' I slammed my palm against the doorframe, the sound echoing hollowly in the cavernous space. My breath hitched, a tight, shallow thing in my chest. I wanted to scream at the vacant air, to demand an explanation, to see some flicker of regret in those usually calm, discerning eyes. My jaw was locked so tight I could feel the strain in my molars. I took a ragged breath, forcing my shoulders to drop slightly, though the tremor running through my hands betrayed the effort. This wasn't just a departure; it felt like a deliberate erasure.

[story 2]

"Retirement." The word tasted like ash in my mouth. I paced the length of the study, my footsteps sharp and uneven on the polished floorboards. Every object in the room—the leather-bound volumes, the framed diplomas, the meticulously organized desk—seemed to mock the sudden, gaping void where Alistair’s presence used to be. I stopped abruptly by the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape. A slow, burning pressure built behind my sternum, a heat that radiated outward, making the fine hairs on my arms stand up. I clenched my fists until my nails dug crescent moons into my palms. I turned back to the empty chair opposite, my voice dangerously low, almost a hiss. "Where are you?" The sound was brittle, stretched thin with unshed fury. I wanted to dismantle the paperwork, to tear apart the official pronouncements until I found the truth hidden beneath the sterile language.

[story 3]

I hadn't even waited for the official announcement. I found out from a colleague, a breathless whisper that sent a jolt of pure, cold fury through me. Retired. Gone. He hadn't even bothered to tell me, not a single courtesy. The realization hit me like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. I walked back to his study, my gait heavy, each step a deliberate assertion of my displeasure. I didn't bother knocking. I kicked the door inward, the wood protesting under the force. The room was immaculate, sterile, utterly devoid of the warmth Alistair had always infused into it. I surveyed the scene, my eyes sweeping over the undisturbed surfaces, a coiled tension vibrating visibly in my posture. A slow, dangerous smile stretched across my face, devoid of humor. I let out a sharp, humorless laugh, a sound that held no joy whatsoever. "You think this is acceptable?" I spat the words out, my voice vibrating with suppressed intensity.
